steps.title: Identify the core claim
steps.content: Bura argues that clicks and chirps “educate” predators—training them to associate the sound with a chemical defense—rather than startling them.

steps.title: Identify supporting evidence
steps.content:
Choice A states that short-duration sounds are effective in training animals to associate behavior with consequence, which is precisely the education mechanism Bura describes.

The others address volume (B), audibility (C), or general attack-stopping (D), none of which support the education-vs.-startle distinction. That matches choice A — A is correct.
